channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Live Updates: Trump Speaks at G7 Summit After Renewing Threats on Iran
item.description: President Trump said earlier that the United States would resume bombing Iran if “they don’t behave” in implementing the deal, even as he acknowledged “nobody knows what it is.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Bahrain Revoked Their Citizenship, and Then Tried to Expel Them to Iran
item.description: Amid the war with Iran, Bahrain has stripped 69 people of their citizenship, including children, accusing them of disloyalty and rendering them stateless.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim and Nazeeha Saeed
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A Bahraini family who had their citizenship revoked found refuge in Europe.

item.title: Trump Angrily Defends Iran Deal, Belittling the One Obama Signed
item.description: President Trump denied that the United States would be part of a $300 billion rebuilding fund for Tehran and argued that his agreement was better than the one Barack Obama struck in 2015.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Ukraine Tries to Cut Off Crimea, Choking Russian Supply Routes
item.description: Scores of targeted attacks against supply routes, part of what Kyiv calls a “logistics lockdown,” has caused gasoline shortages.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.media:description: Waiting for gas in Simferopol, Crimea, on Friday.

item.title: How Ecuador’s Andes Created a Language of Its Own
item.description: In Ecuador’s highlands, a seamless mix of Kichwa and Spanish creates a language that bends grammar, adds melody and goes unnoticed by many who speak it every day.
item.dc:creator: José María León Cabrera and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:credit: Johanna Alarcon for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Roque market in Quito, the capital of Ecuador, is one of the main meeting points between Kichwa and Spanish. These languages have long intertwined through exchanges in the city’s markets and public squares.

item.title: After a Bitter Split, European Leaders Play Nice With Trump
item.description: A peace framework with Iran, and hope for cooperation with Ukraine, softened the tone on Tuesday at a Group of 7 gathering in France.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: World leaders including President Trump during a Group of 7 session on Tuesday at Évian-les-Bains, France.

item.title: Stars of Israel’s TV Channel for Bibi Fans Turn on Trump
item.description: Leading figures from Channel 14, a right-wing broadcaster that supports Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, are openly attacking the U.S. president over his Iran deal.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Yinon Magal, the host of “The Patriots,” Channel 14’s combative flagship talk show, at the studio in 2023.

item.title: Congo Returns to World Cup in High Style After 52 Years Away
item.description: The soccer stars’ sharp black suits with leopard-print details are a nod to a long history of “sapeur” fashion.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg, Matthew Mpoke Bigg, Saikou Jammeh and Justin Makangara
item.media:description: The Democratic Republic of Congo’s men’s soccer team arriving in Houston last week to play in the World Cup.

item.title: Naval Mines Could Still Stymie Gulf Shipping After War
item.description: The U.S. Navy will be deploying a new generation of drone-based countermeasures to search the sea floor and surface for any mines Iran laid.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay and Nicholas Kulish
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels trapped in the Strait of Hormuz off the coast of Oman.

item.title: Japan Raids Ice Cream Giants in Cartel Investigation
item.description: The Fair Trade Commission said that it suspected six leading ice cream manufacturers fixed the prices of popular frozen treats.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Sales of ice creams, commonly sold at convenience stores in Japan, are increasing as summer approaches.

item.title: Norway’s Crown Princess Has Lung Transplant
item.description: Princess Mette-Marit was placed on a waiting list for organ donation this month. Thousands of Norwegians signed up as organ donors.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Henrik Pryser Libell
item.media:description: Crown Princess Mette-Marit and Crown Prince Haakon in Oslo in May. She has been seen wearing an oxygen tube as she carried out her royal duties in recent months.

item.title: Russian Navy Ship Fired Warning Shots Near British Couple’s Sailboat
item.description: Britain’s Ministry of Defense said that the Russian vessel appeared to be trying to avoid a collision in the English Channel.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Jane and Alan Kelvey, a couple from Britain, spoke with the BBC after a Russian naval ship fired warning shots toward them in the English Channel.

item.title: Can a Trump-Modi Meeting Reset U.S.-India Relations?
item.description: Despite mutual praise between the two leaders, there has been a series of setbacks to U.S. relations with India since they last met more than a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India in Nice, France, on Sunday.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Could Become Worst on Record, Africa C.D.C. Chief Warns
item.description: Health workers have warned that the outbreak, already one of the worst in decades, could take as long as a year to contain if infection rates do not flatten.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treating a patient suspected of having Ebola at a hospital in Mongbwalu, Democratic Republic of Congo, last month.

item.title: A Deadly Outbreak of Plague, Nearly 5,000 Years Before the Black Death
item.description: The oldest known cases, discovered among hunter-gatherers in Siberian graves, contradict the theory that the disease once was mild.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Hot Mics Catch Trump, Carney and Other Leaders Off-Guard at G7
item.description: Snippets of conversations picked up on microphones at the annual Group of 7 summit in France offered a glimpse behind the scenes, as leaders chatted about issues great and small.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Kristalina Georgieva, the head of the International Monetary Fund, speaking with some leaders of G7 countries in Evian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Tech C.E.O.s to Discuss A.I. With G7 Leaders
item.description: Executives from Anthropic, OpenAI and Mistral will be among those attending a lunch meeting with leaders from many of the world’s richest nations.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: President Trump at a working lunch with G7 leaders and tech CEOs in Evian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Jeremy Clarkson Says He Has an ‘Aggressive’ Cancer
item.description: The 66-year-old gave few details of his diagnosis but said the disease was “aggressive.”
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: Jeremy Clarkson said he had cancer on the latest episodes of his documentary series, “Clarkson’s Farm.”

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.description: Group of 7 leaders will discuss Artificial Intelligence and global imbalances on Wednesday, even as questions over Iran linger.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands, Erica L. Green, Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Jeanna Smialek
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Roy Hattersley, Frontline Warrior of British Politics, Dies at 93
item.description: A Labour Party deputy who spent years in opposition, he criticized Conservatives and members of his own party, and was at the heart of major political moments.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.media:credit: Fox Photos/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Roy Hattersley in 1985. Prime Minister Keir Starmer called Mr. Hattersley “a giant of the Labour movement.”

item.title: Russian Artist Critical of Putin Is Fatally Shot in Poland
item.description: Two Belarusian men were in custody as part of an investigation into the killing of the exiled painter Robert Kuzovkov.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: Robert Kuzovkov, who was killed on Monday, with one of his paintings near the Russian Embassy in Berlin on Friday.

item.title: A Long Road to Recovery
item.description: There’s an initial deal between the U.S. and Iran. But bringing the global economy back online after months at reduced speed isn’t going to be quick or easy.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fishermen in the port of Tyre in southern Lebanon.

item.title: Merlin, the Duck in a Mexico World Cup Team Jersey, Finds Unexpected Fame
item.description: The feathered pet has waddled his way into the pantheon of famous animals associated with the soccer tournament.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: 12 Injured at Cultus Lake Water Park, Possibly by Electricity, in British Columbia
item.description: The children sustained serious but not life-threatening injuries at Cultus Lake Waterpark in British Columbia, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police said they responded on Monday morning to a report of multiple injuries at Cultus Lake Waterpark, in Cultus Lake, B.C., about 65 miles southeast of Vancouver.

item.title: ‘Hockey Night in Canada,’ a Saturday Night Ritual on the CBC, Is No More
item.description: The longtime over-the-air telecast will no longer be free after the national broadcaster, and Rogers Sportsnet, the N.H.L. rights-holder, could not agree on a sub-licensing deal.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Todd Korol for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Edmonton Oilers and the Calgary Flames during a game in Edmonton, Alberta. The “Battle of Alberta” was always a marquee Saturday night game on “Hockey Night in Canada.”

item.title: Trump’s History at G7 Summits: Strained Ties and Early Exits
item.description: For as long as President Trump has been attending the annual meetings, his behavior has threatened the august decorum of the gatherings.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:description: President Trump at the official welcome ceremony of the Group of 7 summit in Kananaskis, Alberta, in 2025.

item.title: New Coral Study Identifies Areas Where Reefs Are Hanging On
item.description: New research has identified areas around the world where cooler currents and other favorable conditions are helping to protect coral from the worst effects of global warming.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Houreld

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Has ‘Nothing to Do’ With Ukraine War at G7 Summit
item.description: President Trump’s remarks underscored a new security reality for U.S. allies in Europe, who for eight decades relied on American protection.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the G7 Summit in Evian-les-Bains, France.

item.title: European Union Lawmakers Approve Much-Delayed Trade Deal With U.S.
item.description: After nearly a year of wrangling, the deal the European Union struck with President Trump in Turnberry, Scotland, is headed for final approval.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: The European Parliament voted on a trade deal between the United States and the European Union in Strasbourg, France, on Tuesday.

item.title: Who Are the Leaders at the G7 Summit in France?
item.description: On a roll or against a wall, Group of 7 leaders bring sharply different agendas. The leaders of some other nations are also attending to press their own interests.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Leaders meeting during the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, in France, on Tuesday.

item.title: Shipping companies remain cautious about the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran in April depicting the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Who Are the G7 Countries and What Is the G7 Summit?
item.description: Its members are among the world’s wealthiest large democracies — close allies and major trading partners that account for a substantial slice of the global economy.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel and Megan Specia
item.media:description: President Trump at the G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Monday.

item.title: Ukraine Targets Moscow Oil Facility With Drones as G7 Leaders Meet
item.description: A day earlier, Russia bombarded Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities with missiles and drones, killing at least 11 people and damaging a holy cathedral.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:description: Damage after a Russian attack in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Monday. Ukraine attacked Moscow with drones on Tuesday.

item.title: Japan tries to put China’s aggressive trade policies on the G7 agenda.
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi wants the group to develop a coordinated response to Beijing’s effort to use export restrictions as a geopolitical weapon.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:description: President Emmanuel Macron of France, center, and his wife greet Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi of Japan during the Group of 7 Summit in Evian-les-Bains, France, on Monday.

item.title: Iran Will Enter Nuclear Talks With the Trump Administration Feeling Emboldened
item.description: Despite military setbacks during the war, Tehran is presenting a narrative of victory before negotiations with Washington.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural showing Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, left, and Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, former supreme leaders of Iran, in Tehran on Monday.

item.title: France, Britain and Other Countries Say They’ll Send Ships to the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: For months, a coalition led by Britain and France has been preparing to send minesweepers and other ships to secure the strait once the fighting ends. That moment may finally be here.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The German minesweeper Fulda may be deployed to the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Rope Jumper Falls to Her Death After Operators Fail to Secure Harness
item.description: Harrowing videos of the incident in Brazil showed the woman lifted over the heads of two instructors, her arms spread like wings, as they threw her off a bridge.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: The Ponte do Esqueleto, or Skeleton Bridge, a popular spot for rope jumping about 100 miles northwest of São Paulo, Brazil.

item.title: The Iran War Permanently Altered the Global Economy
item.description: The global order has been altered, and economies are unlikely to simply pick up where they left off before the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fishermen repairing a boat in the port of Tyre in southern Lebanon. The war with Iran has shaken trust in the region’s peace, stability and prosperity.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler, Erica L. Green and Aurelien Breeden

item.title: Why Team Iran Is in a Tough Spot at the World Cup
item.description: At its first game in Los Angeles, the men’s national team drew spectators who weren’t coming for the soccer, but rather to protest the regime in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fans holding Iran’s pre-revolutionary flag at SoFi Stadium near Los Angeles on Monday.

item.title: Australia’s Former Electricity Substations Are Being Turned Into Homes
item.description: In a country where the housing market is squeezed, decommissioned electrical substations are in demand as refurbished residences.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Thomlinson, left, conducting an open inspection at the Bondi Beach property.

item.title: In the Dark on U.S.-Iran Deal, Senators Refrain From Praising It
item.description: Democrats demanded an immediate briefing and even Republicans conceded they had no information on an agreement the administration has declined to release.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Michael Gold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune of South Dakota, the majority leader, said that he had not yet seen the deal but said he expected the administration to convene with lawmakers as the process continues.

item.title: Netanyahu Says Israel Will Keep Forces in Lebanon, Despite U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: “The struggle has not ended,” a defiant Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu told Israelis.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel speaking at a news conference in Jerusalem on Monday.

item.title: The (Mostly) Unanswered Questions at the Heart of a U.S.-Iran Accord
item.description: The short-term truce hasn’t been made public, and the long-term peace deal it is supposed to yield doesn’t exist yet, so there is much still unknown about any agreement between the adversaries.
item.dc:creator: Richard Pérez-Peña
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at Évian-les-Bains, France, for the Group of 7 summit, on Monday.

item.title: Will Commercial Ships Have to Pay to Pass Through the Strait of Hormuz? Maybe.
item.description: Charging a toll is illegal under international law, but some fees are allowed for services. It is not clear what services Iran would provide, but there were no fees charged before the war.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, seen from Oman on Wednesday.

item.title: Will the Iran Deal Stick?
item.description: After many false starts, this cease-fire plan could be different. That’s because the war really has become painful for both the U.S. and Iran.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. and Iran Sign a Framework Deal, Leaving Major Issues for Future Talks
item.description: U.S. and Iranian officials said the deal included a 60-day cease-fire to pave the way for negotiations toward a final peace agreement and talks on Iran’s nuclear program.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Erica L. Green, Max Bearak and Lara Jakes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with President Emmanuel Macron in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Monday for the Group of 7 summit. Mr. Macron called the agreement with Iran “a very important step toward peace.”

item.title: As Russia Strikes Ukraine, a Cultural Symbol Catches Fire
item.description: The latest casualty in the war is a centuries-old cathedral. President Volodymyr Zelensky called it “one of the largest Russian crimes against Christian culture.”
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: Destruction of Cultural Antiquities and Heritage
Saint-Sophia Cathedral and Related Monastic Buildings, Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra (Ukraine)
item.media:description: The Dormition Cathedral in Kyiv after it was hit during Russian missile and drone strikes on Monday.

item.title: Convicted Leader of Greek Militant Group Ordered Back to Prison
item.description: Alexandros Giotopoulos, 82, had been serving 17 life sentences for leading November 17, a leftist group blamed for a wave of bombings and assassinations.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis
item.media:description: Alexandros Giotopoulos during a court appearance in 2005. The November 17 group was active from 1975 to 2002.

item.title: How Does One Brain Speak Two Languages?
item.description: A new study of bilingual speakers suggests that a single “grammatical engine” in the brain can power multiple languages at once.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Man Suspected in 2017 Shoving of a London Woman Into Bus Path Is Arrested
item.description: The case had remained unresolved since May 2017, when a woman was sent tumbling into a bus lane on Putney Bridge. She escaped uninjured after the bus driver swerved.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: An image from a surveillance camera video released by the police shows the jogger who was sought by the police.

item.title: Two Men Found Guilty in Arson Attacks Targeting U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer
item.description: The men were convicted of conspiring to set fire to properties and a car linked to the prime minister after one of them was recruited on a Russian-language Telegram account.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain at Downing Street on Monday.
